results.question: What other exercises can help John with his basketball performance?
results.prediction:
From my notes, John already uses strength training (which improved his shooting, agility and speed), does yoga, and is doing daily physical therapy for an ankle injury. To build on that and target his goals (better shooting, explosiveness, injury prevention and consistency), these exercises are especially useful — grouped by purpose with short rationale and simple frequency guidance.

Power & vertical explosiveness
- Box jumps, depth jumps, broad jumps — improve reactive power and jump height. (1–2×/week)
- Power cleans or kettlebell swings — develop hip extension speed for explosive drives. (1–2×/week)
- Trap‑bar or hex‑bar jumps / loaded jump squats — combine load and speed for force production. (1×/week)

Single‑leg strength & stability (critical after ankle injury)
- Bulgarian split squats, single‑leg RDLs, step‑ups — build unilateral strength and balance. (2×/week)
- Single‑leg calf raises and eccentric calf lowering — strengthen the Achilles/ankle complex. (daily/alternate days while rehabbing)
- Single‑leg balance on a BOSU or wobble pad (progress to eyes‑closed) — proprioception for ankle stability. (daily)

Lateral quickness & change of direction
- Lateral bounds, carioca, zig‑zag cone shuffles, T‑drill/agility ladder work — improve cutting and defensive slides. (2×/week)
- Deceleration drills (controlled plant‑and‑stop) — teach safer, quicker stopping and reduce re‑injury risk. (1–2×/week)

Basketball‑specific strength & core
- Medicine‑ball rotational throws & slams — build rotational power for improved drive-and-pass and stronger shots. (2×/week)
- Pallof presses, anti‑rotation chops, farmer carries — improve core stability for shooting consistency and contact resilience. (2–3×/week)
- Weighted walking lunges and hip thrusts — strengthen glutes for explosiveness and stability. (2×/week)

Conditioning & on‑court endurance
- High‑intensity interval sprints (court‑length), shuttle runs, sled pushes/pulls — sport‑specific conditioning and repeated sprint ability. (2–3×/week)
- Jump rope — low‑impact plyometric conditioning that also improves footwork. (3–4×/week short sets)

Mobility, flexibility & recovery (to sustain training volume)
- Ankle and hip mobility drills, thoracic rotations, hamstring/quad dynamic stretches — maintain range for better mechanics. (daily)
- Yoga (he already does this) + foam rolling and targeted soft‑tissue work — aids recovery and balance. (2–4×/week)

Injury prevention & rehab integration
- Nordic hamstring negatives (eccentric hamstring strengthening) — reduce posterior chain injury risk. (1–2×/week)
- Progressive proprioception & plyometric return‑to‑play sequence coordinated with his PT — start low‑impact and progress volume/intensity gradually.

Sample weekly outline (fits around daily practice)
- 2 full lower‑body strength sessions (include single‑leg work)
- 1 power/plyometric session (low volume)
- 2 conditioning/HIIT sessions
- Daily mobility + PT/proprioception work
- Yoga 1–2×/week for flexibility/recovery

results.question: What kind of yoga for building core strength might John benefit from?
results.prediction:
Quick context I’ll use: John already enjoys Warrior II (holds it 30–60s), is using yoga to build strength/flexibility/focus, and is doing daily physical therapy for an injury — so recommendations should boost core strength while staying rehab-safe.

What would help most
- Styles: Vinyasa/Power or short Ashtanga-style flows for dynamic core work; Iyengar or slower Hatha for alignment-focused, rehab-friendly strength; Pilates-informed yoga if he wants targeted core conditioning.
- General cue: emphasize navel-to-spine (draw the belly toward the spine), steady breathing (exhale to engage), and quality alignment over long holds. Check with his PT before trying arm balances or deep backbends.

High-impact core-building poses and variations (with easy mods)
- Plank (high plank and forearm plank) — mod: drop to knees.
- Side plank (Vasisthasana) — mod: bottom knee down or forearm side plank.
- Boat pose (Navasana) and half-boat (Ardha Navasana) — mod: hands behind thighs or knees bent.
- Chaturanga/Low plank (slow lowers) — mod: push-ups on knees, or do negative lowers from high plank.
- Dolphin plank / forearm plank shoulder taps (anti-rotation work).
- Warrior III (Virabhadrasana III) — great for standing-core balance; use a block or wall for support.
- Revolved Chair / Twisted Chair (Parivrtta Utkatasana) — core + anti-rotation strength.
- Locust (Salabhasana) and Bridge lifts with single-leg variations for posterior chain/core.
- Crow prep (knees to triceps) — advanced; only after PT approval and adequate wrist/shoulder strength.

Sample 12–15 minute core-focused sequence John could do 2–3×/week
1. Warm-up (2–3 min): Cat-Cow, Downward Dog → plank x2 (5–10 breaths each).
2. Main (10 min):  
   - Forearm plank 30–45s × 2 (or 3 sets of 20–30s if new) with 20s rest  
   - Boat pose 30s × 3 (hands can be behind thighs)  
   - Side plank 20–30s each side × 2  
   - Revolved Chair 3 × 20–30s (slow, controlled twists)  
   - Warrior III balance hold 20–30s each side × 2
3. Cool-down (2 min): Bridge 3 slow lifts, supine twist, deep belly breaths.

Progression tips
- Increase hold time by 5–10s every 1–2 weeks, or add dynamic reps (knee-to-elbow from plank, slow lowers from plank to chaturanga).
- Add instability once stable: single-leg planks, slow boat-to-boat pulses, or a small fitness ball.
- Integrate with his strength training days (yoga core on lighter cardio/skill days) and avoid exhausting core on heavy lifting days.

Safety notes
- Because John does daily PT, run advanced moves and any sharp pain by his PT/physio first. Favor alignment-focused variations and avoid forcing range of motion that conflicts with rehab guidance.

What an ornithologist does (brief)
- Studies bird behavior, ecology and populations; conducts field surveys and banding; analyzes data for conservation; may teach or work with land managers and NGOs to protect habitat.

Practical steps Andrew can take

4. Build experience: look for positions as a field technician, wildlife rehabilitator, or in citizen-science projects to gain hours and contacts.  
5. Network: attend local conservation meetings, volunteer with land trusts, and connect with university labs or NGOs.
